questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Firedamp, the explosive gas encountered in coal mines, consists predominantly of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Firedamp is the seam gas of coal.

**Reasoning.** It is mainly methane (CH₄) desorbed from coal; methane is combustible and forms explosive mixtures with air, hence the hazard. Small amounts of higher hydrocarbons may accompany it.

**Answer.** Methane.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Because methane has a specific gravity of about 0.55 (lighter than air), it tends to accumulate
options: at the floor of the roadway
near the roof and in roof cavities/rise workings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Buoyancy governs where a gas layers.

**Reasoning.** Being lighter than air, methane rises and collects in roof cavities, the upper part of inclined (rise) headings and behind roof falls — locations that must be specially tested and ventilated.

**Answer.** Near the roof / roof cavities.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane is explosive in air between about 5% (lower limit) and 15% (upper limit). The width of this explosive range, in percentage points, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Range width $=$ upper limit $-$ lower limit.

**Working.**
$$\Delta=15-5=10$$

**Answer.** 10 percentage points.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A firedamp–air mixture detonates most violently at a methane concentration of about
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Maximum violence is near the stoichiometric mixture.

**Reasoning.** Complete combustion of methane needs roughly a 1:2 ratio with oxygen, corresponding to about 9.5–9.8% CH₄ in air; here the explosion pressure and flame temperature peak.

**Answer.** About 9.5–9.8%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Blackdamp (chokedamp) is
options: an irrespirable mixture mainly of carbon dioxide and nitrogen that extinguishes a flame
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Blackdamp is oxygen-deficient air.

**Reasoning.** It is air depleted of oxygen and enriched in CO₂ and N₂ (e.g. from oxidation of coal/timber); it does not support flame or life, hence 'choke' damp.

**Answer.** CO₂ + N₂ irrespirable mixture.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Carbon monoxide (whitedamp) is extremely dangerous chiefly because it
options: smells strongly and warns the miner
combines with haemoglobin (forming carboxyhaemoglobin) with an affinity far greater than oxygen, causing asphyxiation
is heavier than air and settles harmlessly
questions.solution:
**Concept.** CO blocks oxygen transport in blood.

**Reasoning.** CO binds haemoglobin about 200–300 times more readily than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin and starving tissues of oxygen; it is colourless and odourless, giving no warning.

**Answer.** Forms carboxyhaemoglobin (high affinity).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 8-hour TLV-TWA (threshold limit value) for carbon monoxide is commonly taken as ____ ppm.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** TLV-TWA caps average exposure over a work shift.

**Reasoning.** The widely used 8-hour time-weighted average limit for CO is 50 ppm; brief excursions and short-term limits are higher but bounded.

**Answer.** 50 ppm.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Hydrogen sulphide (stinkdamp) in mines is characterised by
options: no smell and being lighter than air
a rotten-egg odour, high toxicity, and being heavier than air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** H₂S is a toxic, dense, malodorous gas.

**Reasoning.** It smells of rotten eggs at low concentration (but deadens the sense of smell at high concentration), is denser than air so collects low down, and is acutely toxic.

**Answer.** Rotten-egg smell, toxic, heavier than air.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 8-hour TLV-TWA for hydrogen sulphide is commonly taken as ____ ppm.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** H₂S is far more toxic than CO₂.

**Reasoning.** Its 8-hour TLV-TWA is about 10 ppm, with a short-term limit around 15 ppm; concentrations of a few hundred ppm are rapidly fatal.

**Answer.** 10 ppm.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Afterdamp is
options: the fresh intake air to a mine
the mixture of gases (rich in CO and CO₂, depleted in O₂) remaining after a fire or explosion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Afterdamp follows combustion events.

**Reasoning.** After an explosion or fire the atmosphere contains high CO and CO₂ with little oxygen; this afterdamp is responsible for most fatalities in mine explosions, as victims survive the blast but succumb to the gases.

**Answer.** Post-explosion CO/CO₂-rich, O₂-poor gas.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Taking the molecular weight of CO₂ as 44 and that of air as 28.97, the specific gravity of carbon dioxide relative to air is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific gravity $=\dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}}$ at equal conditions.

**Working.**
$$SG=\frac{44}{28.97}=1.52$$

**Answer.** 1.52 (heavier than air).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Carbon dioxide in mine air, being heavier than air, tends to
options: rise to the roof like methane
accumulate in dips, sumps and low/old workings and act as an asphyxiant
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dense gases settle in low ground.

**Reasoning.** CO₂ (SG ≈ 1.52) collects in dip workings, sumps and abandoned low areas; it stimulates breathing at low levels but is an asphyxiant as oxygen is displaced.

**Answer.** Accumulates in low workings; asphyxiant.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A flame safety lamp is used underground to
options: detect methane (by the gas cap on the flame) and indicate oxygen deficiency
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The flame responds to gas and to oxygen content.

**Reasoning.** Methane forms a pale-blue 'cap' above the flame whose height indicates its percentage, while a falling flame warns of oxygen deficiency; the gauze prevents the flame igniting the surrounding atmosphere.

**Answer.** Detect methane and oxygen deficiency.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The oxygen content of normal fresh atmospheric air is about ____ % by volume (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Composition of dry air.

**Reasoning.** Fresh air contains about 20.9% oxygen, 78% nitrogen and ~0.04% CO₂; mine air quality is judged against this baseline.

**Answer.** 20.9%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A modern methanometer measures methane concentration by
options: catalytic (pellistor) oxidation of methane on a heated catalyst, sensed as a resistance change
measuring the rotation of an anemometer vane
the colour change of a wet-bulb thermometer
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Catalytic combustion sensing.

**Reasoning.** Methane burns on a heated catalytic bead (pellistor); the heat raises its resistance proportionally to the gas %, giving a direct CH₄ reading. (Infrared sensors are also used.)

**Answer.** Catalytic (pellistor) oxidation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A flame safety lamp flame is extinguished when the oxygen content of the air falls below about
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A flame needs sufficient oxygen.

**Reasoning.** Below roughly 16% O₂ the lamp flame can no longer be sustained and goes out — a warning of dangerous oxygen deficiency, even though such air can still be briefly breathed.

**Answer.** About 16%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Regulations generally require the oxygen content of air in working places not to fall below about ____ % by volume.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A statutory minimum oxygen level.

**Reasoning.** Mine regulations typically require at least about 19% oxygen in places where persons work or travel, providing a safe margin above the level at which impairment begins.

**Answer.** 19%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) in underground air originates mainly from
options: diesel engine exhaust and blasting fumes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NOx is a combustion/blasting product.

**Reasoning.** Diesel equipment and detonation of explosives generate oxides of nitrogen; NO₂ is a brown, highly irritant and toxic gas affecting the lungs, requiring good ventilation of headings.

**Answer.** Diesel exhaust and blasting fumes.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 8-hour TLV-TWA for nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) is commonly taken as ____ ppm.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NO₂ is toxic at low concentration.

**Reasoning.** Its 8-hour TLV-TWA is about 3 ppm (with a short-term limit around 5 ppm), reflecting its strong pulmonary irritancy.

**Answer.** 3 ppm.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) in mine air is typically associated with
options: the combustion or blasting of sulphide-bearing ore/coal, giving a pungent irritant gas
questions.solution:
**Concept.** SO₂ comes from sulphur oxidation.

**Reasoning.** Burning or blasting sulphide ores/high-sulphur coal releases SO₂, a pungent, acidic, irritant gas that attacks the eyes and respiratory tract.

**Answer.** Combustion/blasting of sulphide ore.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane is released into mine workings mainly by
options: desorption of gas from the coal seam and surrounding strata as they are exposed and relieved of pressure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal stores adsorbed methane.

**Reasoning.** Mining relieves the confining pressure, so methane desorbs from the freshly exposed coal and broken strata and emits into the airways; the rate rises with gas content and production.

**Answer.** Desorption from coal/strata on pressure relief.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A seam has an in-situ gas content of 10 m³ per tonne and is mined at 1000 t/day (assume all gas is released). The daily methane emission, in m³/day, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Emission $=$ gas content $\times$ production.

**Working.**
$$Q=10\times1000=10000\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Answer.** 10000 m³/day.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Under typical coal-mine regulations, work must stop and power be cut when the methane concentration in the general body of air at the face reaches about
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Statutory action limits keep CH₄ well below the explosive 5%.

**Reasoning.** A common rule withdraws persons / stops electrical equipment at about 1.25% CH₄ in the general body of air, with the return-airway limit lower (~0.75%), providing a wide safety margin below 5%.

**Answer.** About 1.25%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane is emitted at 2 m³/min into a heading. To dilute it to 1% by volume in the air stream, the required airflow, in m³/min, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Concentration $=\dfrac{Q_{gas}}{Q_{air}}$ (for small concentrations).

**Working.**
$$Q_{air}=\frac{Q_{gas}}{c}=\frac{2}{0.01}=200\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Answer.** 200 m³/min.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Inertisation of a sealed-off fire area with nitrogen or CO₂ controls the fire by
options: lowering the oxygen content below the level that supports combustion and explosion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Combustion needs oxygen above a threshold.

**Reasoning.** Injecting inert gas dilutes the oxygen in the sealed zone below the level needed for burning/explosion, suppressing the fire without water and reducing explosion risk.

**Answer.** Reduces oxygen below the combustion threshold.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Coward's explosibility (triangle) diagram is used to
options: show whether a mixture of methane, air and inert gas is explosive, non-explosive or potentially explosive on dilution
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Explosibility depends on fuel and oxygen together.

**Reasoning.** Coward's diagram plots CH₄ vs effective oxygen and bounds the explosive zone; a sample's point shows if it is safe, explosive, or would become explosive if mixed with air — vital when sealing/reopening fire areas.

**Answer.** Classify CH₄/air/inert mixtures by explosibility.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: If the return air contains 0.30% CH₄ and the face action limit is 1.25% CH₄, the available margin before action is required, in percentage points, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Margin $=$ limit $-$ current value.

**Working.**
$$\Delta=1.25-0.30=0.95$$

**Answer.** 0.95 percentage points.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane (gas) drainage — pre-draining the seam through boreholes before/while mining — is practised primarily to
options: increase the calorific value of the coal
reduce the methane emission into the working airways, lowering explosion risk and ventilation demand
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Capturing gas at source eases ventilation.

**Reasoning.** Draining methane through boreholes (and often utilising it) cuts the volume entering the workings, so less air is needed to dilute it and the explosion hazard falls — essential in highly gassy seams.

**Answer.** Reduce methane emission into the workings.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Spontaneous heating (spontaneous combustion) of coal is initiated by
options: the exothermic low-temperature oxidation of coal, whose heat accumulates when it cannot be dissipated
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal slowly oxidises and releases heat.

**Reasoning.** Oxygen reacts with exposed coal exothermically; if ventilation supplies oxygen but cannot carry the heat away, the temperature climbs progressively to ignition — spontaneous combustion.

**Answer.** Self-heating from exothermic coal oxidation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Which condition MOST promotes spontaneous heating of coal?
options: A high-rank anthracite seam in a dry, well-swept airway
Crushed, low-rank, high-pyrite coal left in the goaf with just enough air leakage to supply oxygen but not remove heat
Strong through-ventilation that removes all heat
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Heating needs reactive coal, oxygen supply and poor heat removal.

**Reasoning.** Friable low-rank coal with pyrite has a large reactive surface; loose coal in the goaf with slow air leakage gets oxygen for oxidation but the trickle of air cannot carry away the heat, so it accumulates.

**Answer.** Crushed low-rank/pyritic coal in a leaky goaf.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 'incubation period' of a coal in relation to spontaneous combustion is
options: the time the explosion flame travels along the airway
the time from first exposure/oxidation of the coal to the onset of an active fire (or a defined temperature rise)
the burning time of a safety fuse
the life of a flame safety lamp battery
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Incubation period indexes proneness to heating.

**Reasoning.** It is the interval between exposure of coal to air and the development of an active fire; a short incubation period denotes a highly susceptible seam needing rapid extraction and sealing.

**Answer.** Time from exposure to onset of fire.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In the crossing-point temperature (CPT) test for spontaneous-combustion susceptibility, a coal with a LOWER crossing-point temperature is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** CPT is where the coal's self-heating overtakes the furnace temperature.

**Reasoning.** A coal that reaches the crossing point at a lower temperature oxidises and self-heats more readily, indicating greater susceptibility to spontaneous combustion.

**Answer.** More prone to spontaneous heating.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: An early indication of incipient spontaneous heating underground is
options: a fall in carbon monoxide to zero
the appearance of carbon monoxide, a rise in CO make, sweating of surfaces and a musty/oily smell
a sudden drop in air temperature
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Oxidation produces CO and moisture before flames appear.

**Reasoning.** Rising CO concentration (and CO make), condensation/sweating on surfaces, a haze and a characteristic musty/oily odour are the recognised early warnings of heating, allowing action before open fire.

**Answer.** CO appearance, sweating and musty smell.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Graham's ratio (the CO index) used to detect spontaneous heating is essentially
options: the ratio of methane produced to dust generated
the ratio of carbon monoxide produced to the oxygen absorbed (expressed as a percentage)
the ratio of intake to return airflow
the ratio of wet-bulb to dry-bulb temperature
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Heating consumes O₂ and yields CO.

**Reasoning.** Graham's ratio $=100\times\dfrac{\text{CO produced}}{\text{O}_2\ \text{absorbed}}$; a rising ratio signals intensifying oxidation/heating and is a key fire-monitoring index.

**Answer.** CO produced ÷ O₂ absorbed (×100).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: An air sample shows 0.05% CO produced against a 2.5% oxygen deficiency. Graham's ratio $\left(100\times\dfrac{\text{CO}}{\text{O}_2\ \text{deficiency}}\right)$ is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $GR=100\times\dfrac{\text{CO}}{\Delta\text{O}_2}$.

**Working.**
$$GR=100\times\frac{0.05}{2.5}=2.0$$

**Answer.** 2.0 (an elevated value suggesting active heating).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Direct (active) attack on a mine fire is appropriate when
options: the fire is small, accessible and can be safely reached and extinguished with water/foam/extinguishers
the fire is large and inaccessible behind goaf
explosive gas has already accumulated heavily
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Method choice depends on accessibility and gas risk.

**Reasoning.** A small, reachable fire with safe gas conditions is best tackled directly using water, foam or extinguishers; large, gassy or inaccessible fires require indirect methods (sealing/inertisation).

**Answer.** Small, accessible fires.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The principal hazard while sealing off (and later reopening) a fire area is
options: the formation of an explosive gas mixture as oxygen falls/rises through the explosive range
the cooling of the mine air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** During sealing/reopening the atmosphere passes through explosibility.

**Reasoning.** As oxygen is depleted (sealing) or admitted (reopening), the CH₄/air/inert mixture can transit the explosive zone in the presence of a hot fire — the most dangerous moment, managed using explosibility diagrams and remote sealing.

**Answer.** Risk of forming an explosive mixture.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The lower explosive limit of a cloud of fine coal dust in air is of the order of ____ g/m³.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A minimum dust concentration is needed to propagate an explosion.

**Reasoning.** Fine bituminous coal dust becomes explosible above roughly 50 g/m³ of air; below this the cloud is too lean to sustain flame propagation.

**Answer.** ~50 g/m³.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A coal-dust explosion in a mine is typically
options: initiated by a firedamp ignition or blast that raises and ignites deposited coal dust, then propagates along the airways
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal dust explosions usually follow a primary ignition.

**Reasoning.** A firedamp flame or blown-out shot raises settled coal dust into a cloud and ignites it; the explosion then feeds on further dust along the roadways, becoming far-reaching and devastating.

**Answer.** Firedamp/blast raises and ignites deposited dust.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Stone dusting (rock dusting) of coal-mine roadways prevents coal-dust explosions by
options: mixing incombustible dust (e.g. limestone) with the coal dust so the cloud cannot propagate an explosion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Inert dust quenches the flame.

**Reasoning.** Spreading limestone dust raises the incombustible fraction of the roadway dust; when raised into a cloud, the inert particles absorb heat and dilute the coal dust below the level that can sustain an explosion.

**Answer.** Adds incombustible dust to suppress propagation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In the absence of firedamp, the minimum incombustible content of roadway dust required to render it non-explosive is commonly specified as about ____ %.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A statutory minimum incombustible fraction.

**Reasoning.** Regulations typically require at least about 65% incombustible content (higher where firedamp is present) so that the dust cloud cannot propagate an explosion.

**Answer.** About 65%.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A roadway has 100 kg of (combustible) coal dust. To achieve a mixture with 80% incombustible content, the mass of incombustible stone dust that must be added, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Incombustible fraction $=\dfrac{\text{stone dust}}{\text{coal}+\text{stone dust}}$.

**Working.** Let added stone dust $=x$.
$$\frac{x}{100+x}=0.80\Rightarrow x=80+0.8x\Rightarrow 0.2x=80\Rightarrow x=400\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 400 kg.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Stone-dust or water-trough explosion barriers installed in roadways are designed to
options: increase the airflow to the face
be dislodged by the pressure wave ahead of an explosion flame, releasing inert dust/water that quenches and arrests the flame
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Barriers suppress a propagating explosion.

**Reasoning.** The pressure pulse preceding the flame tips the loaded shelves/troughs, dispersing stone dust or water into the path of the advancing flame so it is cooled and extinguished before it can spread further.

**Answer.** Release inert dust/water to quench the flame.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A firedamp explosion is especially dangerous in a dusty coal mine because it can
options: raise and ignite deposited coal dust, triggering a far more violent and far-reaching secondary coal-dust explosion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A small gas ignition can escalate via dust.

**Reasoning.** The blast and flame of even a minor firedamp ignition can entrain settled coal dust and set off a coal-dust explosion that propagates throughout the workings — historically the cause of the worst mine disasters.

**Answer.** Trigger a secondary coal-dust explosion.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A seam is reported to have an incubation period of 8 weeks. Expressed in days (1 week = 7 days), this is ____ days.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Convert weeks to days.

**Working.**
$$8\times7=56\ \text{days}$$

**Answer.** 56 days.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: When directly fighting an underground fire, a key precaution is to
options: reverse the main ventilation immediately in every case
maintain controlled ventilation, approach from the intake (fresh-air) side and prevent accumulation of explosive gas behind the firefighters
seal the area while persons are still inside
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Firefighter safety depends on fresh air and gas control.

**Reasoning.** Attacking from the intake keeps firefighters in fresh air with the smoke carried away; ventilation must be controlled to avoid building explosive mixtures or spreading the fire, with continuous gas monitoring.

**Answer.** Keep ventilation, attack from the intake side.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A sealed-off fire area should be reopened only
options: as quickly as possible regardless of conditions
after gas analysis confirms the fire is fully extinguished and cooled, and reopening is done gradually under close monitoring
by admitting a large volume of air at once
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Reopening can re-ignite a smouldering fire or pass through explosibility.

**Reasoning.** Sampling (CO, CO₂, O₂, CH₄, temperature) must show the fire is dead and cool; air is then admitted slowly and monitored to avoid re-igniting embers or forming an explosive mixture.

**Answer.** Only after gas analysis confirms it is safe, done gradually.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Coal A has a crossing-point temperature of 120 °C and coal B 160 °C. The difference in their crossing-point temperatures, in °C, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Difference of the two CPT values.

**Working.**
$$160-120=40\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

(The lower-CPT coal A is the more susceptible.)

**Answer.** 40 °C.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A fire involving energised electrical equipment should NOT be fought with
options: a jet of water (which conducts electricity and risks electrocution)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Water conducts electricity.

**Reasoning.** Energised electrical fires must be tackled with non-conducting agents (CO₂, dry powder) after isolating the supply; a water jet risks electrocution and equipment damage.

**Answer.** A water jet.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A fire emits carbon monoxide at 0.5 m³/min. To dilute it to 0.005% (50 ppm) by volume in the air stream, the required airflow, in m³/min, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q_{air}=\dfrac{Q_{gas}}{c}$.

**Working.**
$$Q_{air}=\frac{0.5}{0.00005}=10000\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Answer.** 10000 m³/min.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Spontaneous-combustion risk is generally greatest in
options: thin anthracite seams with strong ventilation
thick, low-rank, high-sulphur seams worked by caving methods that leave loose coal in the goaf
fully stowed workings with no loose coal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose reactive coal plus oxygen leakage drives heating.

**Reasoning.** Thick low-rank, pyritic seams mined by caving leave broken coal in the goaf where slow air leakage feeds oxidation — the classic high-risk setting; stowing or high extraction reduces the loose coal and the risk.

**Answer.** Thick low-rank high-sulphur caved seams.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A tube-bundle / continuous gas-monitoring system in a mine is used to
options: draw and analyse air samples (CO, CO₂, O₂, CH₄) from behind seals and key locations to detect heatings/fires early
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Remote sampling gives early warning.

**Reasoning.** Sample lines continuously bring air from sealed areas and workings to a surface analyser; trends in CO, CO₂, O₂ and CH₄ (and ratios like Graham's) reveal developing heatings before they become fires.

**Answer.** Continuous remote gas analysis for fire detection.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: If Graham's ratio at a sealed area rises from 0.5 to 2.0, the factor by which it has increased is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Factor $=\dfrac{\text{new value}}{\text{old value}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{2.0}{0.5}=4$$

**Answer.** 4 (a strong rise indicating intensifying heating).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: An indirect method of dealing with a large, inaccessible underground fire is
options: directing miners to beat out the flames by hand
sealing off the area and, where suitable, flooding it with water or injecting inert gas
increasing the oxygen supply to burn it out faster
removing all stoppings to ventilate it
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Inaccessible fires are isolated and starved of oxygen.

**Reasoning.** Building seals cuts the air supply; the zone may then be flooded with water (if the geometry allows) or inertised with N₂/CO₂ to extinguish the fire safely from a distance.

**Answer.** Sealing plus flooding/inertisation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A small air leakage into a sealed fire area is dangerous because it can
options: supply just enough oxygen to keep the fire smouldering and may bring the gas mixture into the explosive range
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Partial oxygen supply is the worst case.

**Reasoning.** A small leak feeds slow combustion (heat and CO continue) without flushing the area, and can dilute the rich gas behind the seal into the explosive band — hence seals must be as airtight as possible.

**Answer.** Sustains smouldering and risks an explosive mixture.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Which of the following are recognised early indicators of spontaneous heating underground? (Select all that apply.)
options: Rise in temperature of the air and surrounding strata
Appearance of a haze with a musty/oily smell and sweating of surfaces
A sudden increase in the oxygen content of the air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Oxidation gives off CO, heat and moisture and consumes oxygen.

**Reasoning.** Rising CO, higher temperatures, haze/odour and surface sweating all accompany incipient heating, while oxygen is consumed (not increased) — so an oxygen rise is not an indicator.

**Answer.** Options 1, 2 and 3.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The thermal (heat) environment experienced by an underground worker depends on
options: the combination of dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature (humidity), air velocity and radiant heat
only the depth of the mine
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Human heat balance involves several factors.

**Reasoning.** Comfort/heat stress is set by air temperature, humidity (which governs sweat evaporation), air movement (convective/evaporative cooling) and radiant heat from hot surfaces — not temperature alone.

**Answer.** Dry-bulb, wet-bulb, air velocity and radiant heat.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 'effective temperature' (ET) index is
options: an empirical index combining dry-bulb, wet-bulb and air velocity to represent the thermal sensation felt by a worker
the temperature of the rock at depth
the boiling point of mine water
questions.solution:
**Concept.** ET expresses combined thermal effect as one number.

**Reasoning.** It merges temperature, humidity and air speed into an equivalent still-air, saturated temperature giving the same sensation — used to set comfort/working limits underground.

**Answer.** Empirical index of dry-bulb, wet-bulb and velocity.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A high wet-bulb temperature is dangerous in hot mines because it
options: indicates high humidity, which suppresses evaporation of sweat and so impairs the body's cooling
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sweat cooling depends on humidity.

**Reasoning.** The body sheds heat largely by evaporating sweat; when the air is humid (high wet-bulb) evaporation slows, the worker cannot cool down, and heat stress/heat stroke becomes likely.

**Answer.** High humidity suppresses evaporative cooling.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: At a station the dry-bulb temperature is 35 °C and the wet-bulb temperature is 30 °C. The wet-bulb depression, in °C, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Wet-bulb depression $=$ dry-bulb $-$ wet-bulb.

**Working.**
$$35-30=5\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Answer.** 5 °C.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The principal source of heat in a deep mine is usually
options: the geothermal heat of the surrounding rock (rising with the geothermal gradient), supplemented by autocompression and machinery
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rock heat dominates at depth.

**Reasoning.** Strata temperature rises with depth (geothermal gradient), and air heats by autocompression as it descends; machinery, oxidation, men and blasting add further heat. Rock heat is usually the largest contributor in deep mines.

**Answer.** Geothermal rock heat (plus autocompression/machinery).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: With a surface temperature of 25 °C and a geothermal gradient of 30 °C per km, the virgin rock temperature at a depth of 1.5 km, in °C, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rock temperature $=$ surface temperature $+$ gradient $\times$ depth.

**Working.**
$$T=25+30\times1.5=25+45=70\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Answer.** 70 °C.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Taking autocompression to raise dry-bulb temperature by about 1 °C per 100 m of descent, the temperature rise of air going down a 1000 m shaft, in °C, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rise $=$ depth ÷ 100 m (≈1 °C per 100 m).

**Working.**
$$\Delta T=\frac{1000}{100}\times1=10\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Answer.** 10 °C.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A kata thermometer is used in mine-climate work to measure
options: the cooling power of the air (and hence low air velocities/comfort)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The kata measures heat-loss (cooling) rate.

**Reasoning.** The time for the heated kata bulb to cool through a fixed range gives the air's cooling power, from which low air velocities and the comfort of the environment can be assessed.

**Answer.** Cooling power of the air.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Progressive heat-stress disorders in hot mines, in increasing order of severity, are broadly
options: heat stroke, then heat exhaustion, then heat cramps
heat cramps, then heat exhaustion, then heat stroke (a medical emergency)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Heat illness escalates with thermal load.

**Reasoning.** Salt/water loss first causes heat cramps, then heat exhaustion (faintness, collapse), and finally heat stroke where thermoregulation fails — a life-threatening emergency. Control is by cooling, ventilation, hydration and work-rest regimes.

**Answer.** Cramps → exhaustion → heat stroke.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: An air stream of mass flow 50 kg/s is to be cooled by 5 °C. Taking $c_p=1.005$ kJ/kg·K, the refrigeration (cooling) duty, in kW, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\dot{Q}=\dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T$.

**Working.**
$$\dot{Q}=50\times1.005\times5=251.25\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 251.25 kW.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 'respirable' fraction of airborne dust, responsible for pneumoconiosis, consists of particles
options: larger than 50 µm that settle quickly
fine enough (broadly below about 5–7 µm) to penetrate deep into the alveoli of the lungs
visible to the naked eye only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Only fine particles reach the gas-exchange region.

**Reasoning.** Coarse dust is trapped in the upper airways, but particles of roughly a few micrometres penetrate to the alveoli where they are retained, causing fibrotic lung disease.

**Answer.** Fine particles (~<5–7 µm) reaching the alveoli.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis (CWP) and silicosis are both
options: occupational lung diseases (fibrosis) caused by the inhalation and retention of mineral dust — coal dust and free silica respectively
caused by exposure to methane gas
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dust diseases are non-infectious fibrotic disorders.

**Reasoning.** Long-term inhalation of coal dust causes CWP, and of free crystalline silica causes silicosis; both lead to lung fibrosis and reduced respiratory function and are irreversible.

**Answer.** Dust-induced occupational lung fibrosis.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Silicosis is caused specifically by inhalation of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Free silica is the fibrogenic agent.

**Reasoning.** Quartz (free crystalline SiO₂) dust, common where siliceous rock is drilled/cut, produces silicosis; this is why silica has a much lower exposure limit than coal dust.

**Answer.** Free crystalline silica (quartz).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 8-hour TLV-TWA for respirable crystalline silica is of the order of ____ mg/m³ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Silica has a very low permissible limit.

**Reasoning.** Because quartz is strongly fibrogenic, the respirable silica TLV-TWA is about 0.1 mg/m³ — far below the limit for nuisance or coal dust.

**Answer.** ~0.1 mg/m³.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A gravimetric respirable-dust sampler determines dust concentration by
options: drawing a known air volume through a size-selector (cyclone) and weighing the dust collected on a filter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Concentration $=$ mass collected ÷ air volume.

**Reasoning.** A pre-selector (cyclone) passes only the respirable fraction to a weighed filter; the mass gain divided by the metered air volume gives mg/m³ — the standard exposure measure.

**Answer.** Weigh filtered respirable dust per air volume sampled.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The cyclone fitted ahead of the filter in a respirable-dust sampler serves to
options: remove the coarse, non-respirable particles so that only the respirable fraction reaches the filter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Size selection mimics the human airway.

**Reasoning.** The cyclone spins out and discards the larger particles that would be trapped in the upper airways, letting through the fine fraction that penetrates the lungs — so the filter mass reflects the health-relevant respirable dust.

**Answer.** Reject coarse particles; pass the respirable fraction.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A sampler's filter gains 0.6 mg of dust after sampling 300 litres (0.3 m³) of air. The respirable dust concentration, in mg/m³, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Concentration $=\dfrac{\text{mass collected}}{\text{air volume}}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{0.6}{0.3}=2.0\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Answer.** 2.0 mg/m³.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The most effective primary measure to control airborne dust generation at a drilling/cutting point is
options: wet drilling / water sprays that suppress the dust at the point of generation
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Suppress dust at source.

**Reasoning.** Adding water at the cutting/drilling point wets the fines so they do not become airborne; combined with ventilation and respirators this is the front-line control. Dry drilling does the opposite.

**Answer.** Wet drilling / water sprays.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Water infusion of a coal seam (pumping water into boreholes ahead of mining) helps control dust by
options: pre-wetting the coal so that less dust becomes airborne when it is cut
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Moisture in the coal reduces dust release.

**Reasoning.** Infused water penetrates cleats and pores; when the coal is later cut, the dampened fines stay bound rather than dispersing into the air, lowering respirable-dust exposure.

**Answer.** Pre-wets the coal to reduce airborne dust.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A measured respirable dust concentration is 6 mg/m³ where the TLV is 3 mg/m³. The factor by which the exposure exceeds the limit is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Exceedance factor $=\dfrac{\text{measured}}{\text{limit}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{6}{3}=2$$

**Answer.** 2 (twice the permissible limit).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The threshold limit value (TLV-TWA) of a contaminant is
options: the concentration that is instantly fatal
the time-weighted average concentration to which a worker may be exposed over a normal 8-hour day/40-hour week without adverse effect
the explosive limit of the gas
the concentration that extinguishes a flame
questions.solution:
**Concept.** TLV-TWA is an occupational exposure benchmark.

**Reasoning.** It represents the average airborne concentration over a working shift believed safe for nearly all workers on repeated exposure; short-term and ceiling limits supplement it.

**Answer.** Safe 8-hour time-weighted average concentration.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Adequate illumination underground is important mainly because it
options: improves safety, visibility and productivity and helps prevent accidents and visual disorders
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Lighting affects safety and performance.

**Reasoning.** Good lighting lets workers see hazards, read instruments and work accurately, reducing accidents and eye strain (historically miner's nystagmus), and raising productivity.

**Answer.** Safety, visibility and productivity.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A luminous flux of 2000 lumens is spread uniformly over a working area of 50 m². The average illuminance, in lux, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Illuminance $=\dfrac{\text{luminous flux}}{\text{area}}$ (1 lux = 1 lm/m²).

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{2000}{50}=40\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** 40 lux.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Miner's nystagmus, an involuntary oscillation of the eyes, was historically associated with
options: prolonged work in poorly illuminated underground conditions
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Nystagmus is a lighting-related visual disorder.

**Reasoning.** Long hours of work under dim, flickering lamp light strained the eyes and were linked to nystagmus; improved cap-lamp and roadway lighting greatly reduced its incidence.

**Answer.** Poor underground illumination.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: To provide an average of 50 lux over a 100 m² work area, the total luminous flux required (ignoring losses), in lumens, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Flux $=$ illuminance $\times$ area.

**Working.**
$$\Phi=50\times100=5000\ \text{lm}$$

**Answer.** 5000 lumens.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Glare in mine lighting is
options: excessive or poorly shielded brightness in the field of view causing visual discomfort and reduced visibility
the same as illuminance in lux
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Glare is uncontrolled brightness contrast.

**Reasoning.** Bright unshielded lamps in the line of sight dazzle the eye and lower the ability to see detail; it is controlled by shielding/diffusing luminaires and positioning them out of direct view.

**Answer.** Discomforting excessive brightness.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A cooling duty of 251.25 kW is required. Taking 1 tonne of refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW, the refrigeration capacity needed, in TR, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{TR}=\dfrac{\dot{Q}\ (\text{kW})}{3.517}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{TR}=\frac{251.25}{3.517}=71.4\ \text{TR}$$

**Answer.** 71.4 TR.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Which of the following are accepted measures to control respirable dust exposure underground? (Select all that apply.)
options: Wet drilling and water sprays at the point of dust generation
Adequate ventilation to dilute and carry away airborne dust
Use of approved respirators/dust masks as a supplementary control
Dry drilling to complete the work faster
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Control at source, by dilution and by protection.

**Reasoning.** Water suppression stops dust becoming airborne, ventilation removes what is generated, and respirators protect the worker where exposure remains. Dry drilling increases dust and is unacceptable.

**Answer.** Options 1, 2 and 3.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Occupational noise exposure is measured in
options: A-weighted decibels, dB(A), which approximate the response of the human ear
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The ear's sensitivity varies with frequency.

**Reasoning.** Sound level meters apply A-weighting to mimic the ear's frequency response, giving dB(A); occupational limits and hearing-damage criteria are expressed in dB(A).

**Answer.** dB(A).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The commonly accepted permissible noise exposure limit for an 8-hour working day is ____ dB(A).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A daily noise dose limit protects hearing.

**Reasoning.** A level of about 90 dB(A) for 8 hours is the widely used permissible exposure limit; higher levels are allowed only for proportionately shorter times.

**Answer.** 90 dB(A).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Noise-induced hearing loss in miners is
options: a permanent sensorineural hearing loss from prolonged exposure to high noise, preventable by noise control and hearing protection
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Chronic loud noise damages cochlear hair cells.

**Reasoning.** Repeated high-level noise destroys hair cells, causing permanent (irreversible) hearing loss; it is prevented by reducing noise at source and wearing ear protection.

**Answer.** Permanent sensorineural loss, preventable.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For a point source in free field the level drops 6 dB each time the distance doubles. If the level at 10 m is 100 dB, the level at 20 m, in dB, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Inverse-square law: $-6$ dB per distance doubling.

**Working.**
$$L_{20}=100-6=94\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** 94 dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Using a 3 dB exchange rate with 90 dB(A) permitted for 8 h, the permissible exposure time at 93 dB(A), in hours, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each +3 dB halves the allowed time.

**Working.**
$$93=90+3\Rightarrow t=\frac{8}{2}=4\ \text{h}$$

**Answer.** 4 hours.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Prolonged use of hand-held percussive drills can cause
options: hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS / vibration white finger)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Localised vibration damages blood vessels/nerves of the hand.

**Reasoning.** Hand-transmitted vibration from drills/breakers causes HAVS (vibration white finger), with blanching, numbness and reduced grip; whole-body vibration from vehicles causes back disorders instead.

**Answer.** Hand-arm vibration syndrome.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The preferred hierarchy for controlling occupational noise is
options: ear protection first, then everything else
control at the source, then along the transmission path (enclosure/barrier), and only then protection at the receiver
ignore the source and rely solely on distance
increase ventilation to disperse the noise
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Engineering controls precede personal protection.

**Reasoning.** Reducing noise at the machine (source) is most effective; if residual noise remains, enclosures/barriers attenuate the path; hearing protection at the worker is the last resort.

**Answer.** Source → path → receiver.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Acid mine drainage (AMD) is generated when
options: pyrite (FeS₂) in the rock is oxidised in the presence of water and air, producing sulphuric acid and dissolved metals
methane is drained from a coal seam
diesel exhaust mixes with mine air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sulphide oxidation acidifies mine water.

**Reasoning.** Exposure of pyrite to oxygen and water yields sulphuric acid and ferrous/ferric sulphate, dropping the pH and mobilising heavy metals — the origin of acidic, metal-laden mine discharges.

**Answer.** Oxidation of pyrite by air and water.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Acid mine water at pH 3 is to be neutralised to pH 7. The increase in pH required, in pH units, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Change $=$ target pH $-$ initial pH.

**Working.**
$$\Delta\text{pH}=7-3=4$$

**Answer.** 4 pH units (achieved by adding lime/limestone).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A common treatment for acid mine drainage is
options: neutralisation with lime/limestone to raise pH and precipitate the dissolved metals (often with settling/wetlands)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Raise pH to drop out metals.

**Reasoning.** Alkaline reagents (lime, limestone) neutralise the acidity; the rise in pH precipitates iron and other metals as hydroxides, which settle out in ponds or constructed wetlands.

**Answer.** Lime/limestone neutralisation and metal precipitation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Mining subsidence is
options: the rise of the surface above a mine
the downward movement/settlement of the ground surface caused by extraction of the underlying mineral
the collapse of a haul-truck tyre
the heating of coal in the goaf
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Removing support lets the overburden settle.

**Reasoning.** As underground voids close (e.g. longwall caving), the strata sag and the surface subsides, forming a subsidence trough that can damage structures, services and drainage.

**Answer.** Surface settlement from underground extraction.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For a subsidence factor of 0.6 and an extracted seam thickness of 3 m, the maximum possible surface subsidence, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Max subsidence $=$ subsidence factor $\times$ seam thickness.

**Working.**
$$S_{max}=0.6\times3=1.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 1.8 m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 'angle of draw' in subsidence engineering is
options: the slope angle of the final highwall
the angle from the vertical at the panel edge to the limit of perceptible surface subsidence
the angle of repose of the spoil
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It defines the lateral extent of the subsidence basin.

**Reasoning.** Measured from the vertical at the edge of the extracted panel, the angle of draw locates where surface movement effectively ceases; a larger angle means subsidence extends further beyond the workings.

**Answer.** Vertical-to-limit-of-subsidence angle at the panel edge.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For an angle of draw of 35° and a working depth of 200 m, the horizontal distance from the panel edge to the limit of subsidence, in m, is ____ (nearest whole number). [tan 35° = 0.700]
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Limit distance $=$ depth $\times\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

**Working.**
$$d=200\times\tan35^{\circ}=200\times0.700=140\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 140 m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Reclamation (restoration) of mined-out opencast land typically involves
options: leaving the spoil heaps untouched permanently
backfilling and regrading, replacing topsoil and revegetating the land for a productive after-use
flooding the entire region with acid water
increasing the depth of the pit indefinitely
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Restore land to a stable, usable condition.

**Reasoning.** Spoil is regraded to stable slopes, topsoil is respread and vegetation/agriculture or forestry re-established, returning the disturbed land to beneficial use and controlling erosion.

**Answer.** Regrade, replace topsoil and revegetate.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: An Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) for a mining project is
options: a financial audit of the mine's profits
a systematic study to predict, evaluate and propose mitigation for the environmental and social effects of the project before approval
questions.solution:
**Concept.** EIA informs environmental decision-making.

**Reasoning.** It identifies likely impacts on air, water, land, ecology and communities, assesses their significance and sets out mitigation/monitoring (the environmental management plan) so authorities can decide on the project.

**Answer.** Pre-project study of environmental effects and mitigation.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The dominant air-pollution problem from opencast mining is
options: fugitive dust (particulate matter) from drilling, blasting, loading and haulage, controlled by water spraying and green belts
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mechanical operations generate airborne dust.

**Reasoning.** Drilling, blasting, material handling and truck haulage raise particulate matter; control measures include water spraying of roads, dust extraction, and perimeter plantation/green belts.

**Answer.** Fugitive particulate dust.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Mine water discharged to surface streams commonly requires treatment to remove
options: suspended solids, acidity and heavy metals (e.g. via settling ponds and neutralisation)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mine effluent carries solids, acidity and metals.

**Reasoning.** Sediment-laden, sometimes acidic and metal-bearing water is passed through settling ponds (to drop solids) and neutralisation (to raise pH and precipitate metals) before discharge to meet standards.

**Answer.** Suspended solids, acidity and heavy metals.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A spherical silt particle of 20 µm diameter (density 2650 kg/m³) settles in water (density 1000 kg/m³, viscosity 0.001 Pa·s). Its Stokes settling velocity, in mm/s, is ____ (two decimals). [use $v=\dfrac{g d^2(\rho_p-\rho_w)}{18\mu}$]
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stokes' law for a small settling sphere.

**Working.**
$$v=\frac{9.81\times(20\times10^{-6})^2(2650-1000)}{18\times0.001}$$
$$=\frac{9.81\times4\times10^{-10}\times1650}{0.018}=3.6\times10^{-4}\ \text{m/s}=0.36\ \text{mm/s}$$

**Answer.** 0.36 mm/s.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A green belt (plantation) developed around a mine helps mainly by
options: trapping airborne dust, attenuating noise, improving aesthetics and acting as a carbon sink
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vegetation buffers environmental impacts.

**Reasoning.** A belt of trees intercepts and settles dust, attenuates noise, stabilises soil and absorbs CO₂, while improving the visual environment — a standard mitigation measure.

**Answer.** Dust/noise capture, aesthetics and carbon sink.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Two equal, incoherent noise sources each produce 90 dB at a point. Their combined level (adding 3 dB for a doubling of energy), in dB, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Doubling sound energy adds 3 dB.

**Working.**
$$L=90+10\log_{10}2=90+3=93\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** 93 dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In India, the statutory body responsible for enforcing safety and health regulations in mines is the
options: Directorate General of Mines Safety (DGMS), under the Mines Act
questions.solution:
**Concept.** DGMS administers mine safety law.

**Reasoning.** The DGMS enforces the Mines Act and associated regulations (safety, health, gas/dust limits, inspections), distinct from environmental regulators such as the Pollution Control Boards.

**Answer.** DGMS.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Standard personal protective equipment for an underground coal miner includes
options: a safety helmet with cap lamp, self-rescuer, ear protection, dust respirator and safety boots
a flame safety lamp worn as a necklace
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PPE guards against multiple hazards.

**Reasoning.** The miner needs head protection and light (helmet + cap lamp), an escape breathing device (self-rescuer), hearing and respiratory protection, and protective footwear — addressing impact, gas, noise and dust risks.

**Answer.** Helmet/cap lamp, self-rescuer, ear and dust protection, boots.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A self-contained self-rescuer (SCSR) carried by miners is designed to
options: provide a supply of breathable oxygen so the wearer can escape an irrespirable/CO-laden atmosphere after a fire or explosion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Escape breathing apparatus.

**Reasoning.** An SCSR generates or supplies oxygen independent of the surrounding air, letting a miner breathe while travelling out through smoke, CO or oxygen-deficient air following an emergency.

**Answer.** Supplies breathable oxygen for escape.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: With a 3 dB exchange rate and 90 dB(A) permitted for 8 h, the permissible exposure time at 96 dB(A), in hours, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each +3 dB halves the allowed time.

**Working.**
$$96=90+2\times3\Rightarrow t=\frac{8}{2^2}=\frac{8}{4}=2\ \text{h}$$

**Answer.** 2 hours.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Which of the following are environmental impacts associated with mining? (Select all that apply.)
options: Acid mine drainage and surface-water pollution
A guaranteed improvement in regional groundwater quality
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mining disturbs land, water and air.

**Reasoning.** Excavation and subsidence degrade land, sulphide oxidation acidifies water (AMD), and operations raise dust — all adverse impacts. Mining does not inherently improve groundwater quality; if anything it can degrade it.

**Answer.** Options 1, 2 and 3.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In the settling pond shown, a particle settles with a terminal velocity of $0.01\ \mathrm{m/s}$ and must fall through the full water depth of 2 m. The minimum settling (retention) time, in seconds, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='90' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='210' y2='30' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='80' cy='45' r='4' fill='#b45309'/><line x1='80' y1='52' x2='80' y2='110' stroke='#b45309' stroke-dasharray='3 2' marker-end='url(#sdn)'/><defs><marker id='sdn' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#b45309'/></marker></defs><line x1='214' y1='30' x2='214' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='150' y='80' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>depth 2 m</text><text x='92' y='82' font-size='9'>v = 0.01 m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Particle settling in a pond

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Settling time $t=\dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{settling velocity}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{2}{0.01}=200\ \text{s}$$

**Answer.** $200$ s.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point noise source (see figure) produces a sound level of 90 dB at a distance of 1 m. Assuming spherical spreading (inverse-square attenuation, $L_2=L_1-20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$), the level at 10 m, in dB, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='10' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='40' y='112' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>source</text><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='22' fill='none' stroke='#f59e0b'/><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='34' fill='none' stroke='#f59e0b'/><circle cx='220' cy='80' r='6' fill='#2563eb'/><text x='220' y='112' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>receiver</text><line x1='52' y1='80' x2='212' y2='80' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='110' y='72' font-size='9'>r2 = 10 m</text><text x='8' y='52' font-size='8'>90 dB @ 1 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Noise spreading from a point source

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a point source, level drops 20 dB per decade of distance.

**Working.**
$$L_2=90-20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)=90-20\times1=70\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $70$ dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Workings lie at a depth of 100 m and the angle of draw is $30^\circ$ (see figure). Measured from a point vertically above the edge of extraction, the horizontal distance to the limit of subsidence at the surface, in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='40' x2='250' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><path d='M10 40 C90 40 100 90 130 90 C160 90 170 40 250 40' fill='none' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='105' y='128' width='50' height='14' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='130' y='154' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>extraction</text><line x1='130' y1='128' x2='130' y2='44' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='134' y='100' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>H = 100 m</text><line x1='130' y1='40' x2='70' y2='40' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='34' y='34' font-size='8' fill='#16a34a'>angle of draw 30°</text></svg>
figure.caption: Subsidence trough and angle of draw

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal limit $=H\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

**Working.**
$$d=100\tan30^\circ=100\times0.5774=57.74\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $57.74$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The figure shows a roadway in which a gas has accumulated as a layer against the roof, above the ventilating air stream. The gas most likely responsible is

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='80' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='18' fill='#fca5a5'/><text x='120' y='44' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#991b1b'>gas layer</text><line x1='40' y1='80' x2='200' y2='80' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#afn)'/><defs><marker id='afn' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='110' y='98' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>airflow</text></svg>
figure.caption: Gas layered at the roof of a roadway

options: methane (CH₄), which is lighter than air and collects at the roof
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Roof layering indicates a gas lighter than air.

**Reasoning.** Methane has a specific gravity of about 0.55, so it rises and forms layers along the roof where ventilation is weak; CO₂ and H₂S are denser than air and tend to collect near the floor.

**Answer.** Methane (CH₄).

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane is emitted into a roadway at $0.1\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ (see figure). To keep the general-body concentration at or below $1\%$ by volume, the minimum airflow required, in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='8' y1='65' x2='40' y2='65' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ain)'/><line x1='200' y1='65' x2='236' y2='65' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ain)'/><defs><marker id='ain' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><circle cx='120' cy='88' r='5' fill='#b45309'/><text x='120' y='82' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>CH4 0.1 m^3/s</text><text x='78' y='52' font-size='9'>dilute to 1%</text></svg>
figure.caption: Diluting methane in a roadway

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Required airflow $Q=\dfrac{\text{gas emission rate}}{\text{allowable fractional concentration}}$ (approximately, since the gas volume is small compared with the air).

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{0.1}{0.01}=10\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $10\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A spherical dust particle of diameter $20\ \mu\text{m}$ and density $2500\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ settles in still air ($\mu = 1.8\times10^{-5}\ \mathrm{Pa\,s}$). Neglecting air buoyancy and taking $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$, compute the Stokes terminal settling velocity in $\mathrm{mm/s}$.

figure.caption: Dust particle settling in still air
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='90' cy='50' r='8' fill='#b45309'/><line x1='90' y1='62' x2='90' y2='130' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#sd)'/><defs><marker id='sd' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='30' y='152' font-size='8'>d=20 micron, settling in air</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stokes' law: $v=\dfrac{d^2(\rho_p-\rho_a)g}{18\mu}$, with $\rho_a$ neglected.

**Working.**
$$v=\frac{(20\times10^{-6})^2\times2500\times9.81}{18\times1.8\times10^{-5}}=0.0303\ \mathrm{m/s}=30.3\ \mathrm{mm/s}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 30.3\ \mathrm{mm/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Two machines each producing a sound pressure level of $90\ \text{dB}$ operate together (incoherent sources). Compute the combined sound level in dB.

figure.caption: Two equal noise sources
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='50' width='40' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='110' y='50' width='40' height='40' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='34' y='110' font-size='8'>90 dB</text><text x='114' y='110' font-size='8'>90 dB</text><text x='55' y='30' font-size='8'>combined level?</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Doubling the acoustic energy raises the level by $10\log_{10}2\approx3\ \text{dB}$.

**Working.**
$$L=90+10\log_{10}(2)=90+3.01=93.0\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 93\ \text{dB}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point noise source gives $100\ \text{dB}$ at $1\ \text{m}$. Under free-field (inverse-square) conditions, compute the sound level at $2\ \text{m}$ in dB.

figure.caption: Inverse-square decay with distance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='10' fill='#dc2626'/><g fill='none' stroke='#94a3b8'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='40'/><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='80'/></g><circle cx='70' cy='70' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='110' cy='70' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='52' y='95' font-size='8'>1 m</text><text x='92' y='95' font-size='8'>2 m</text><text x='20' y='132' font-size='8'>100 dB at 1 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** In a free field the level falls $6\ \text{dB}$ per doubling of distance ($20\log_{10}2$).

**Working.**
$$L=100-20\log_{10}\left(\tfrac{2}{1}\right)=100-6.02=94.0\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 94\ \text{dB}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A noise standard permits $8\ \text{h}$ exposure at $90\ \text{dB(A)}$ and halves the permissible time for every $3\ \text{dB}$ increase. Compute the permissible exposure (hours) at $96\ \text{dB(A)}$.

figure.caption: Permissible exposure vs level
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='180' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polyline points='40,30 80,55 120,80 160,95' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='128' font-size='8'>dB vs permissible hours</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each $3\ \text{dB}$ rise halves the allowed time.

**Working.**
$$90\to8\ \text{h};\ 93\to4\ \text{h};\ 96\to2\ \text{h}$$

**Answer.** $2$ hours.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A flat seam lies at depth $H = 200\ \text{m}$. With an angle of draw of $35^\circ$ (measured from the vertical), compute the horizontal distance (m) from a panel edge to the limit of surface subsidence.

figure.caption: Angle of draw over a panel edge
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='90' y='120' width='60' height='14' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='90' y1='120' x2='55' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='90' y1='120' x2='90' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='2 2'/><text x='95' y='82' font-size='8'>H=200 m</text><text x='30' y='150' font-size='8'>angle of draw=35 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The subsidence limit is offset horizontally by $H\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

**Working.**
$$x=200\tan35^\circ=200\times0.7002=140.04\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 140\ \text{m}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For a critical-width extraction, the maximum subsidence equals the subsidence factor times the extracted seam thickness. With subsidence factor $a = 0.6$ and seam thickness $m = 2\ \text{m}$, compute the maximum subsidence (m).

figure.caption: Maximum subsidence over critical area
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><path d='M20 40 Q120 110 220 40' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='90' y='120' width='60' height='12' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='40' y='145' font-size='8'>a=0.6, seam m=2 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $S_{\max}=a\,m$.

**Working.**
$$S_{\max}=0.6\times2=1.2\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $1.2$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A seam is worked at depth $H = 300\ \text{m}$ with an angle of draw of $25^\circ$. Compute the critical width of extraction (m) required for full subsidence, given critical width $=2H\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

figure.caption: Critical width of extraction
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='120' width='80' height='14' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='80' y1='120' x2='45' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='160' y1='120' x2='195' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='95' y='90' font-size='8'>H=300 m</text><text x='30' y='150' font-size='8'>angle of draw=25 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Critical width $W=2H\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

**Working.**
$$W=2\times300\times\tan25^\circ=600\times0.4663=279.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 280\ \text{m}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: At a junction, an airway carrying $Q_1 = 20\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ at $0.2\%$ gas mixes with $Q_2 = 30\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ at $0.7\%$ gas. Compute the resulting gas concentration (\%) in the combined stream.

figure.caption: Mixing of two air streams
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='50' x2='100' y2='74' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#mj)'/><line x1='10' y1='110' x2='100' y2='82' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#mj)'/><line x1='100' y1='78' x2='210' y2='78' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#mj)'/><defs><marker id='mj' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='12' y='44' font-size='8'>Q1=20, 0.2%</text><text x='12' y='128' font-size='8'>Q2=30, 0.7%</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The mixed concentration is the flow-weighted average: $C=\dfrac{Q_1C_1+Q_2C_2}{Q_1+Q_2}$.

**Working.**
$$C=\frac{20(0.2)+30(0.7)}{20+30}=\frac{4+21}{50}=0.5\%$$

**Answer.** $0.5\%$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point light source of luminous intensity $100\ \text{cd}$ illuminates a surface placed normal to the rays at $2\ \text{m}$. Compute the illuminance (lux) on the surface.

figure.caption: Inverse-square law of illumination
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='90' cy='30' r='10' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='90' y1='40' x2='90' y2='128' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><rect x='60' y='128' width='60' height='8' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='95' y='90' font-size='8'>d=2 m</text><text x='40' y='152' font-size='8'>I=100 cd</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Illuminance $E=\dfrac{I}{d^2}$ for a surface normal to the rays.

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{100}{2^2}=25\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** $25$ lux.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The same $100\ \text{cd}$ source now illuminates a surface (normal to the rays) at $4\ \text{m}$. Compute the illuminance (lux).

figure.caption: Illuminance at a larger distance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='90' cy='25' r='9' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='90' y1='34' x2='90' y2='150' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='55' y1='80' x2='125' y2='80' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='140' y2='140' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='128' y='82' font-size='8'>2 m</text><text x='143' y='142' font-size='8'>4 m</text><text x='35' y='20' font-size='8'>I=100 cd</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $E=\dfrac{I}{d^2}$; doubling the distance reduces illuminance to one quarter.

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{100}{4^2}=6.25\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** $6.25$ lux.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Respirable dust enters a heading at $5\ \mathrm{mg/s}$. To keep the airborne concentration at or below $2.5\ \mathrm{mg/m^3}$, compute the minimum air quantity required ($\mathrm{m^3/s}$).

figure.caption: Dilution of airborne dust
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='35' width='200' height='60' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='6' y1='65' x2='40' y2='65' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='80' r='4' fill='#92400e'/><text x='85' y='55' font-size='8'>dust 5 mg/s</text><text x='30' y='115' font-size='8'>limit 2.5 mg/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\dfrac{\text{dust rate}}{\text{allowable concentration}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{5}{2.5}=2\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $2\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Three identical machines, each at $90\ \text{dB}$, run simultaneously. Compute the combined sound pressure level (dB) for these incoherent sources.

figure.caption: Three equal noise sources
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'><rect x='20' y='50' width='35' height='35'/><rect x='90' y='50' width='35' height='35'/><rect x='160' y='50' width='35' height='35'/></g><text x='24' y='105' font-size='8'>90</text><text x='94' y='105' font-size='8'>90</text><text x='160' y='105' font-size='8'>90 dB</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Combined level $=L_1+10\log_{10}(n)$ for $n$ equal incoherent sources.

**Working.**
$$L=90+10\log_{10}(3)=90+4.77=94.77\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 94.8\ \text{dB}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A mine pump delivers $Q = 0.05\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ against a total head of $100\ \text{m}$ at an overall efficiency of $0.70$. Taking $\rho = 1000\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ and $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$, compute the input power (kW).

figure.caption: Mine dewatering pump
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='60' cy='130' r='18' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='60' y1='112' x2='60' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#pp)'/><defs><marker id='pp' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><line x1='60' y1='30' x2='160' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3'/><text x='70' y='82' font-size='8'>H=100 m</text><text x='30' y='162' font-size='8'>Q=0.05 m^3/s, eff=0.7</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Input power $P=\dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$.

**Working.**
$$P=\frac{1000\times9.81\times0.05\times100}{0.70}=70071\ \text{W}\approx70.07\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** $\approx 70.1\ \text{kW}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A dewatering pump must overcome a static lift of $80\ \text{m}$ plus friction losses equivalent to $20\ \text{m}$ of head. Compute the total pumping head (m).

figure.caption: Static lift plus friction head
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='40' y2='30' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='34' y1='80' x2='46' y2='80' stroke='#94a3b8'/><text x='50' y='115' font-size='8'>static=80 m</text><text x='50' y='58' font-size='8'>friction=20 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total head $=$ static head $+$ friction head.

**Working.**
$$H=80+20=100\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $100$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: On the diagram, the shaded band marks the flammable range of methane in air. The approximate lower and upper explosive limits are:

figure.caption: Flammable range of methane in air
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='40' width='180' height='24' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='65' y='40' width='40' height='24' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#b91c1c'/><text x='66' y='80' font-size='8'>5%</text><text x='95' y='80' font-size='8'>15%</text><text x='30' y='30' font-size='8'>methane in air: flammable band</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Methane's explosive (flammable) range in air spans the lower and upper explosive limits.
