**Reasoning.** The mixture ignites only between roughly $5\%$ (LEL) and $15\%$ (UEL); the most violent explosion is near the stoichiometric $\sim9.5\%$.

**Answer.** About $5\%$ to $15\%$ methane.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The gas mixture labelled in the figure (blackdamp / chokedamp) that collects in poorly ventilated workings is chiefly composed of:
options: carbon dioxide and nitrogen, deficient in oxygen
carbon monoxide and hydrogen sulphide only

figure.caption: Composition of blackdamp
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='140' height='50' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='55' y='70' font-size='9'>CO2 + N2</text><text x='35' y='110' font-size='8'>blackdamp (oxygen deficient)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Blackdamp is the residual atmosphere left after oxygen is consumed by oxidation or breathing.

**Reasoning.** It is essentially a non-flammable, oxygen-deficient mixture of carbon dioxide and nitrogen, dangerous because it cannot support life rather than because it explodes.

**Answer.** Carbon dioxide and nitrogen, deficient in oxygen.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In the figure the dashed line from the panel edge to the surface limit of subsidence makes an angle with the vertical. This angle is the:
options: angle of draw, measured from the vertical
angle of repose of the spoil
angle of internal friction of the roof

figure.caption: Definition of the angle of draw
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='110' width='60' height='12' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='80' y1='110' x2='80' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='2 2'/><line x1='80' y1='110' x2='45' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='48' y='78' font-size='8'>angle of draw</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Subsidence extends beyond the worked area; its lateral reach is defined by the angle of draw.

**Reasoning.** The angle of draw is measured between the vertical at the panel edge and the line to the point of zero subsidence on the surface.

**Answer.** Angle of draw, measured from the vertical.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The sampling head shown (a cyclone pre-classifier ahead of a filter) is used to collect respirable dust because it:
options: removes the coarse, non-respirable particles so only the respirable fraction reaches the filter
collects every particle regardless of size
measures gas concentration rather than dust

figure.caption: Cyclone respirable-dust sampler
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='50,30 110,30 90,110 70,110' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='80' y1='110' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='45' x2='50' y2='45' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#cy)'/><defs><marker id='cy' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='20' y='162' font-size='8'>cyclone removes coarse dust</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A cyclone uses centrifugal action to separate particles by aerodynamic size.

**Reasoning.** Coarse particles are spun out and fall to the grit pot, while the respirable fraction (median cut near $4\ \mu\text{m}$) passes to the filter for gravimetric measurement.

**Answer.** It removes coarse, non-respirable particles so only the respirable fraction reaches the filter.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The bar chart shows permissible exposure decreasing as noise level rises. Under the $3\ \text{dB}$ exchange rate, each $3\ \text{dB}$ increase in level:
options: increases the permissible time by 3 hours

figure.caption: Exchange rate for noise exposure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='180' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='50' y='80' width='14' height='30' fill='#94a3b8'/><rect x='90' y='55' width='14' height='55' fill='#94a3b8'/><rect x='130' y='35' width='14' height='75' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='40' y='128' font-size='8'>+3 dB halves exposure</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The exchange rate links an increase in level to a reduction in allowed time at equal noise dose.

**Reasoning.** With a $3\ \text{dB}$ exchange rate the sound energy doubles for every $3\ \text{dB}$, so the permissible exposure time is halved.

**Answer.** It halves the permissible exposure time.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The surface profile drawn above a wide extracted panel is a smooth depression. This subsidence trough has:
options: maximum subsidence near the centre and zero at the margins
maximum subsidence at the margins and zero at the centre

figure.caption: Subsidence trough profile
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><path d='M20 42 Q120 120 220 42' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='140' font-size='8'>subsidence trough profile</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over a critical or super-critical panel the surface forms a subsidence trough.

**Reasoning.** Maximum vertical lowering occurs over the centre of the goaf and tapers to zero at the limit defined by the angle of draw.

**Answer.** Maximum subsidence near the centre and zero at the margins.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The instrument index shown is used to assess underground heat stress. The WBGT index is computed from:

figure.caption: Heat stress thermometer
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='52' y='20' width='16' height='100' rx='8' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='60' cy='130' r='16' fill='#dc2626'/><rect x='56' y='60' width='8' height='70' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='8' y='150' font-size='8'>heat stress index</text></svg>

options: a weighted combination of wet-bulb, globe and dry-bulb temperatures
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) is a composite heat-stress index.

**Reasoning.** It combines natural wet-bulb, globe (radiant) and dry-bulb temperatures (indoors: $0.7T_{wb}+0.3T_{g}$), capturing humidity, radiation and air temperature together.

**Answer.** A weighted combination of wet-bulb, globe and dry-bulb temperatures.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A gravimetric sampler draws $2\ \text{L/min}$ for $120\ \text{min}$ and collects $4\ \text{mg}$ of dust. Compute the dust concentration ($\mathrm{mg/m^3}$, two decimals).

figure.caption: Gravimetric dust sampler
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 170 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='60,40 100,40 90,90 70,90' fill='#dbeafe' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='70' y='90' width='20' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='80' y1='20' x2='80' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='12' y='145' font-size='8'>flow 2 L/min, 120 min</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Concentration $=\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{volume sampled}}$.

**Working.**
$$V=2\times120=240\ \text{L}=0.24\ \mathrm{m^3},\quad C=\frac{4}{0.24}=16.67\ \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $16.67\ \mathrm{mg/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The conical device shown upstream of the filter in a personal dust sampler is a:

figure.caption: Cyclone pre-classifier
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='45,30 105,30 90,100 60,100' fill='#dbeafe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='75' cy='55' r='3' fill='#b45309'/><circle cx='65' cy='72' r='2' fill='#b45309'/><text x='12' y='130' font-size='8'>cyclone separates dust</text></svg>

options: cyclone that selects the respirable dust fraction
device that increases the total dust collected
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Size-selective sampling of airborne dust.

**Reasoning.** A cyclone pre-classifier removes the coarse, non-respirable particles by centrifugal action so that only the respirable fraction reaches the collection filter.

**Answer.** Cyclone that selects the respirable dust fraction.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Two incoherent noise sources each produce $85\ \text{dB}$ at a point (figure). Compute the combined sound level (dB, two decimals).

figure.caption: Two equal noise sources
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='50' cy='60' r='18' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='120' cy='60' r='18' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#475569'/><text x='36' y='64' font-size='8'>85 dB</text><text x='106' y='64' font-size='8'>85 dB</text><line x1='70' y1='60' x2='100' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><text x='28' y='110' font-size='8'>combined level?</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Doubling the acoustic energy adds $10\log_{10}2\approx3\ \text{dB}$.

**Working.**
$$L=85+10\log_{10}2=85+3.01=88.01\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $88.01$ dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point source gives $96\ \text{dB}$ at $1\ \text{m}$. Assuming free-field spherical spreading, compute the level at $2\ \text{m}$ (dB).

figure.caption: Inverse-square decay of sound
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='16' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='100' cy='60' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='170' cy='60' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='26' y='64' font-size='7'>src</text><text x='88' y='50' font-size='8'>1 m</text><text x='150' y='50' font-size='8'>2 m</text><text x='28' y='100' font-size='8'>96 dB at 1 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each doubling of distance reduces the level by $6\ \text{dB}$ ($20\log_{10}2$).

**Working.**
$$L=96-6=90\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $90$ dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Under the OSHA 5-dB exchange rule ($90\ \text{dBA}\Rightarrow8\ \text{h}$), determine the permissible daily exposure time at $95\ \text{dBA}$ (hours).

figure.caption: Level vs permitted exposure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polyline points='30,40 80,40 80,60 130,60 130,80 180,80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='35' y='118' font-size='8'>level vs permitted time</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each $+5\ \text{dBA}$ halves the permissible time.

**Working.**
$$95\ \text{dBA}=90+5\Rightarrow T=\frac{8}{2}=4\ \text{h}$$

**Answer.** $4$ hours.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point source of luminous intensity $200\ \text{cd}$ illuminates a surface $2\ \text{m}$ away, normal to the beam. Compute the illuminance (lux).

figure.caption: Inverse-square illumination
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='14' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><g stroke='#f59e0b'><line x1='54' y1='60' x2='190' y2='30'/><line x1='54' y1='60' x2='190' y2='90'/></g><line x1='110' y1='45' x2='110' y2='78' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='80' y='110' font-size='8'>I=200 cd, d=2 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $E=\dfrac{I}{d^2}$.

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{200}{2^2}=\frac{200}{4}=50\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** $50$ lux.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A working area of $20\ \mathrm{m^2}$ is to be lit to an average illuminance of $100\ \text{lux}$. Compute the luminous flux required (lumens).

figure.caption: Flux over an area
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='12' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><g stroke='#f59e0b'><line x1='110' y1='42' x2='50' y2='100'/><line x1='110' y1='42' x2='170' y2='100'/></g><rect x='40' y='100' width='140' height='12' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><text x='55' y='126' font-size='8'>area 20 m^2 at 100 lux</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\Phi=E\times A$.

**Working.**
$$\Phi=100\times20=2000\ \text{lm}$$

**Answer.** $2000$ lumens.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Carbon monoxide is released at $0.001\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ in a heading. To keep the concentration at or below $50\ \text{ppm}$, compute the minimum airflow required ($\mathrm{m^3/s}$).

figure.caption: Dilution of carbon monoxide
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='35' width='180' height='40' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='6' y1='55' x2='36' y2='55' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='65' r='3' fill='#6b7280'/><text x='78' y='50' font-size='8'>CO 0.001 m^3/s</text><text x='28' y='98' font-size='8'>dilute to 50 ppm</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\dfrac{\text{gas emission}}{\text{allowable fraction}}$, with $50\ \text{ppm}=5\times10^{-5}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{0.001}{5\times10^{-5}}=20\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $20\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For the fine dust particle settling in still air (figure), Stokes' law states that the terminal settling velocity is proportional to:

figure.caption: Particle settling in air
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 150 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='45' y='20' width='60' height='130' fill='#dbeafe' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='75' cy='50' r='5' fill='#6b7280'/><line x1='75' y1='58' x2='75' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#st)'/><defs><marker id='st' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L3 6 L6 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='15' y='162' font-size='8'>particle settling</text></svg>

options: the square of the particle diameter
the inverse of the particle diameter
the cube of the air viscosity
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stokes' law: $v_s=\dfrac{g(\rho_p-\rho_f)d^2}{18\mu}$.

**Reasoning.** The settling velocity varies with $d^2$, so fine particles settle far more slowly than coarse ones and stay airborne longer.

**Answer.** The square of the particle diameter.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A stream of $1\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ carrying $2\%$ of a gas mixes with $9\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ of clean air (figure). Compute the resulting gas concentration (%).

figure.caption: Mixing of two air streams
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M10 40 L80 40 L80 50 L10 50 Z' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M10 70 L80 70 L80 80 L10 80 Z' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='35' width='130' height='50' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><text x='13' y='37' font-size='7'>1 m^3/s @ 2%</text><text x='13' y='100' font-size='7'>9 m^3/s clean</text></svg>

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: For a critical-width panel, the subsidence factor is $a=0.65$ and the extracted seam thickness is $m=3\ \text{m}$. Compute the maximum subsidence (m).

figure.caption: Subsidence trough over a panel
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='40' x2='210' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M40 40 Q110 95 180 40' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='95' x2='160' y2='95' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='25' y='112' font-size='8'>extracted panel, a=0.65, m=3 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $S_{max}=a\times m$.

**Working.**
$$S_{max}=0.65\times3=1.95\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $1.95$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A panel lies at depth $H=250\ \text{m}$ and the angle of draw is $30^\circ$. Compute the horizontal distance from the panel edge to the limit of subsidence (m, two decimals).

figure.caption: Angle of draw geometry
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='30' x2='210' y2='30' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='60' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='160' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='110' y2='30' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='114' y='62' font-size='8'>gamma</text><text x='25' y='125' font-size='8'>H=250 m, draw angle 30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Limit offset $=H\tan\gamma$.

**Working.**
$$d=250\tan30^\circ=250(0.5774)=144.34\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $144.34$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A drainage pump lifts $0.08\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ of water through a total head of $120\ \text{m}$ at an overall efficiency of $0.75$. Compute the input power (kW, two decimals). Take $\rho g=9810\ \mathrm{N/m^3}$.

figure.caption: Mine drainage pump
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='60' cy='100' r='22' fill='#dbeafe' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='60' y1='78' x2='60' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#pp)'/><defs><marker id='pp' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L3 6 L6 0 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='12' y='135' font-size='8'>Q=0.08 m^3/s, H=120 m, eff=0.75</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Input power $=\dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$.

**Working.**
$$P=\frac{9810\times0.08\times120}{0.75}=\frac{94176}{0.75}=125568\ \text{W}=125.57\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** $125.57$ kW.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: In the flame safety lamp shown, the height of the pale blue cap above the flame is used to estimate:

figure.caption: Flame safety lamp
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='45' y='30' width='30' height='90' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='60,40 66,60 54,60' fill='#f59e0b'/><polygon points='60,35 70,60 50,60' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.4'/><rect x='40' y='120' width='40' height='20' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='8' y='160' font-size='8'>flame cap indicates CH4</text></svg>

options: the percentage of methane in the air
the air velocity past the lamp
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Methane burns above the flame, forming a pale-blue cap.

**Reasoning.** The cap height grows with methane content, so a trained observer estimates the percentage of firedamp from the cap above the lowered test flame.

**Answer.** The percentage of methane in the air.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Three incoherent noise sources each producing $80\ \text{dB}$ act together at a point (figure). Compute the combined sound level (dB, two decimals).

figure.caption: Three equal noise sources
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'><circle cx='60' cy='50' r='12'/><circle cx='100' cy='50' r='12'/><circle cx='140' cy='50' r='12'/></g><circle cx='100' cy='95' r='6' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='32' y='115' font-size='8'>three 80 dB sources at a point</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $L=L_1+10\log_{10}n$ for $n$ equal sources.

**Working.**
$$L=80+10\log_{10}3=80+4.77=84.77\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $84.77$ dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A dust sample collects $6\ \text{mg}$ of dust from $3\ \mathrm{m^3}$ of sampled air (figure). Compute the dust concentration ($\mathrm{mg/m^3}$).

figure.caption: Dust sample
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='30' width='90' height='70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#6b7280'><circle cx='70' cy='55' r='2'/><circle cx='95' cy='70' r='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='50' r='2'/><circle cx='85' cy='85' r='2'/></g><text x='42' y='120' font-size='8'>6 mg dust in 3 m^3 air</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Concentration $=\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{volume}}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{6}{3}=2\ \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $2\ \mathrm{mg/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point source of luminous intensity $200\ \text{cd}$ illuminates a surface $4\ \text{m}$ away, normal to the beam (figure). Compute the illuminance (lux).

figure.caption: Inverse-square illumination
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='12' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#475569'/><g stroke='#f59e0b'><line x1='52' y1='60' x2='180' y2='30'/><line x1='52' y1='60' x2='180' y2='90'/></g><text x='32' y='120' font-size='8'>I=200 cd, distance 4 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $E=\dfrac{I}{d^2}$.

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{200}{4^2}=\frac{200}{16}=12.5\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** $12.5$ lux.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A point noise source gives $96\ \text{dB}$ at $2\ \text{m}$ (figure). Using free-field spreading $L_2=L_1-20\log_{10}(d_2/d_1)$, compute the level at $8\ \text{m}$ (dB, two decimals).

figure.caption: Noise spreading with distance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='10' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='none' stroke='#f87171'><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='30'/><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='55'/></g><circle cx='120' cy='55' r='5' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='30' y='110' font-size='8'>96 dB at 2 m, find at 8 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Free-field attenuation $L_2=L_1-20\log_{10}(d_2/d_1)$.

**Working.**
$$L_2=96-20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8}{2}\right)=96-20\log_{10}4=96-12.04=83.96\ \text{dB}$$

**Answer.** $83.96$ dB.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The instrument shown has a water-soaked wick over its bulb (figure). The temperature it reads is:

figure.caption: Wet-bulb thermometer
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='20' width='14' height='90' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='67' cy='118' r='14' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='62' y='95' width='10' height='20' fill='#dc2626'/><path d='M67 104 q14 0 14 14' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb'/><text x='14' y='145' font-size='8'>thermometer with wetted wick</text></svg>

options: the wet-bulb temperature, which with the dry-bulb reflects the cooling power of humid air
the dry-bulb temperature, unaffected by humidity
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Psychrometry in hot mines.

**Reasoning.** Evaporation from the wetted wick cools the bulb to the wet-bulb temperature; combined with the dry-bulb reading it gives humidity and the cooling power of the air.

**Answer.** The wet-bulb temperature, which with the dry-bulb reflects the cooling power of humid air.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A seam of thickness $3\ \text{m}$ is extracted with a subsidence factor of $0.6$ (figure). Compute the maximum possible surface subsidence (m).

figure.caption: Surface subsidence trough
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='180' height='15' fill='#a16207'/><path d='M20 35 Q110 75 200 35' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='100' width='60' height='12' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='32' y='130' font-size='8'>seam m=3, factor a=0.6</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $S_{max}=a\times m$.

**Working.**
$$S_{max}=0.6\times3=1.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $1.8$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A flat seam lies at depth $H=200\ \text{m}$ and the angle of draw is $25^\circ$ (figure). Compute the half-width of the surface influence zone measured from the panel edge (m, two decimals).

figure.caption: Angle of draw
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='180' height='12' fill='#cbd5e1'/><rect x='90' y='120' width='40' height='10' fill='#1f2937'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'><line x1='110' y1='125' x2='60' y2='36'/><line x1='110' y1='125' x2='160' y2='36'/></g><text x='32' y='145' font-size='8'>H=200, draw angle 25 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The influence half-width from a point is $H\tan(\text{angle of draw})$.

**Working.**
$$x=200\tan25^\circ=200\times0.4663=93.26\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $93.26$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A district has $40$ workers, each requiring $0.05\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ of fresh air (figure). Compute the minimum air quantity required ($\mathrm{m^3/s}$).

figure.caption: Per-person air requirement
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='160' height='40' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='60' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='120' cy='60' r='4'/><circle cx='150' cy='60' r='4'/></g><text x='30' y='105' font-size='8'>40 men at 0.05 m^3/s each</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\text{workers}\times\text{rate per worker}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=40\times0.05=2\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $2\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Methane is liberated at $0.06\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ and must be diluted to $1\%$ by volume (figure). Compute the air quantity required ($\mathrm{m^3/s}$).

figure.caption: Methane dilution
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='160' height='45' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='70' cy='62' r='6' fill='#16a34a'/><line x1='90' y1='62' x2='170' y2='62' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='105' font-size='8'>CH4 0.06 m^3/s, dilute to 1%</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\dfrac{\text{gas rate}}{\text{limit fraction}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{0.06}{0.01}=6\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $6\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A worker's exposure varies through the shift as shown (figure). The relevant occupational limit is expressed as:

figure.caption: Variable exposure over a shift
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='40' y='60' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='90' y='40' width='40' height='60'/><rect x='140' y='75' width='40' height='25'/></g><text x='32' y='120' font-size='8'>exposure varies over the shift</text></svg>

options: the single highest instantaneous reading only
the lowest reading recorded during the shift
the arithmetic sum of all readings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Occupational exposure metrics.

**Reasoning.** Variable exposures are compared against the 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA), which averages concentration weighted by the time spent at each level.

**Answer.** The 8-hour time-weighted average concentration.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A working area of $20\ \mathrm{m^2}$ is to be lit to $100\ \text{lux}$ (figure). Compute the total luminous flux required, assuming uniform distribution (lumens).

figure.caption: Area illumination
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='40' width='120' height='60' fill='#fef9c3' stroke='#475569'/><text x='56' y='75' font-size='9'>100 lux over 20 m^2</text><text x='52' y='120' font-size='8'>total flux?</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Flux $=$ illuminance $\times$ area.

**Working.**
$$\Phi=100\times20=2000\ \text{lm}$$

**Answer.** $2000$ lm.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The chart shows that as sound level rises, the permissible daily exposure time falls (figure). Under a $3\ \text{dB}$ exchange rate, the rule is to:

figure.caption: Level versus allowed time
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><polyline points='30,90 90,70 150,50 190,40' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='34' y='120' font-size='8'>dB up, allowed time down</text></svg>

options: halve the permissible exposure time for each 3 dB rise in level
double the permissible exposure time for each 3 dB rise in level
keep the exposure time constant regardless of level
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Noise exchange rate.

**Reasoning.** With a 3 dB exchange rate, every 3 dB increase doubles the sound energy, so the permissible exposure time is halved to keep the dose constant.

**Answer.** Halve the permissible exposure time for each 3 dB rise in level.
